scenario: LLM Foundations: Tokens and Context Window 🧠
text: A language model processes text as {{c1::tokens}}, and the {{c2::context window}} limits how much information it can attend to at once.
explanation: Understanding tokens and context length is essential for prompt design, memory, and cost control.
usage: Critical for long-document QA, chat systems, and retrieval pipelines.
spanish: Un modelo de lenguaje procesa el texto como tokens, y la ventana de contexto limita cuánta información puede atender a la vez.

text: The Transformer uses {{c1::self-attention}} to weigh the relevance of every token relative to the current token.
explanation: Attention is the mechanism that allows models to build rich contextual representations.
usage: Core concept behind modern LLMs, vision transformers, and multimodal models.
spanish: El Transformer usa autoatención para ponderar la relevancia de cada token respecto al token actual.

text: Pretraining teaches the model language structure by predicting missing or next {{c1::tokens}} over massive corpora.
explanation: This is where the model learns general linguistic and factual patterns before task-specific tuning.
usage: Foundational for foundation models and domain adaptation.
spanish: El preentrenamiento enseña al modelo la estructura del lenguaje al predecir tokens faltantes o siguientes sobre corpus masivos.

text: {{c1::Instruction tuning}} improves a model’s ability to follow user requests by training on examples of instruction-response pairs.
explanation: It is a key step between raw language modeling and usable chat assistants.
usage: Widely used in chatbots, copilots, and enterprise assistants.
spanish: El ajuste por instrucciones mejora la capacidad del modelo para seguir solicitudes entrenando con pares instrucción-respuesta.

text: Reinforcement Learning from Human {{c1::Feedback}} aligns models with human preferences using reward models and policy optimization.
explanation: RLHF makes outputs safer, more helpful, and more aligned with user expectations.
usage: Common in general-purpose assistants and preference-based chat systems.
spanish: El aprendizaje por refuerzo con feedback humano alinea los modelos con preferencias humanas usando modelos de recompensa y optimización de política.

text: {{c1::Scaling laws}} suggest that larger models, more data, and more compute often improve performance in predictable ways.
explanation: They help teams reason about performance ceilings and infrastructure tradeoffs.
usage: Useful for model selection and budget planning in LLM projects.
spanish: Las leyes de escalado sugieren que modelos más grandes, más datos y más cómputo suelen mejorar el rendimiento de forma predecible.

text: A {{c1::hallucination}} is a confident but unsupported answer produced by the model.
explanation: Hallucinations are one of the biggest risks in production LLM systems and require grounding and evaluation.
usage: Critical in legal, medical, and enterprise knowledge assistants.
spanish: Una alucinación es una respuesta segura pero no sustentada producida por el modelo.

text: A {{c1::model}} can memorize training examples, but strong generalization requires learning reusable abstractions.
explanation: This distinction is crucial when evaluating whether a system truly understands a task or is just pattern matching.
usage: Important for benchmarks, privacy, and strong deployment practices.
spanish: Un modelo puede memorizar ejemplos de entrenamiento, pero una fuerte generalización requiere aprender abstracciones reutilizables.

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Vaswani et al., 2017))
text: Ecuación para {{c1::Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Vaswani et al., 2017)}}: \[\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(Q\): Query matrix of shape \((B, H, S, d_k)\) representing current tokens.
  • \(K\): Key matrix of shape \((B, H, S, d_k)\) representing context tokens.
  • \(V\): Value matrix of shape \((B, H, S, d_v)\) representing token representation vectors.
  • \(d_k\): Dimensionality of query/key vectors, scaling factor to prevent vanishing gradients in softmax.
spanish: Fórmula de Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Vaswani et al., 2017)

scenario: Code Pattern 💻 (Scaled Dot-Product Attention in PyTorch)
text:
Patrón de código en python para {{c1::Scaled Dot-Product Attention in PyTorch}}:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, mask=None):
    d_k = q.size(-1)
    scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
    attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return torch.matmul(attn_weights, v), attn_weights
explanation: Computes attention scores by dotting query and key matrices, dividing by scale factor \(\sqrt{d_k}\), applying softmax, and multiplying values.
usage:
Snippet: 
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, mask=None):
    d_k = q.size(-1)
    scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
    attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return torch.matmul(attn_weights, v), attn_weights

Output expected: Success
spanish: Patrón de código: Scaled Dot-Product Attention in PyTorch

text: ¿Cómo funciona la estructura de {{c1::Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)}} en el sistema?
explanation:
GQA groups query heads into G groups, where query heads share a single Key-Value (KV) head. This balances the high throughput of Multi-Query Attention (MQA) with the high capacity of Multi-Head Attention (MHA), optimizing KV cache storage during inference (Ainslie et al., 2023).

graph TD subgraph Queries ["Query Heads (Q1 - Q8)"] Q1 --> K1_V1["KV Head 1"] Q2 --> K1_V1 Q3 --> K1_V1 Q4 --> K1_V1 Q5 --> K2_V2["KV Head 2"] Q6 --> K2_V2 Q7 --> K2_V2 Q8 --> K2_V2 end subgraph Keys_Values ["Key/Value Heads (KV1 - KV2)"] K1_V1 K2_V2 end
usage:
Visual model for Grouped-Query Attention (GQA).
Graph:
graph TD subgraph Queries ["Query Heads (Q1 - Q8)"] Q1 --> K1_V1["KV Head 1"] Q2 --> K1_V1 Q3 --> K1_V1 Q4 --> K1_V1 Q5 --> K2_V2["KV Head 2"] Q6 --> K2_V2 Q7 --> K2_V2 Q8 --> K2_V2 end subgraph Keys_Values ["Key/Value Heads (KV1 - KV2)"] K1_V1 K2_V2 end
spanish: GQA agrupa las cabezas de consulta en G grupos, donde las cabezas dentro de un grupo comparten una única cabeza de Clave/Valor (KV), optimizando el almacenamiento del caché KV durante la inferencia.

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (Chinchilla Scaling Laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022))
text: Ecuación para {{c1::Chinchilla Scaling Laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022)}}: \[N \approx 20 \times D\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(N\): Total parameter count of the model (excluding embeddings).
  • \(D\): Total token count used during pretraining.
  • \(20\): The compute-optimal ratio of tokens per parameter established under a fixed compute budget.
spanish: Fórmula de Chinchilla Scaling Laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022)

text: Ecuación para {{c1::Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)}}: \[R_{\Theta, m}^d = \text{diag}\left( R_{\theta_1, m}, R_{\theta_2, m}, \dots, R_{\theta_{d/2}, m} \right) \quad \text{where} \quad R_{\theta_i, m} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos m\theta_i & -\sin m\theta_i \\ \sin m\theta_i & \cos m\theta_i \end{pmatrix}\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(m\): Token position index in the sequence.
  • \(\theta_i = 10000^{-2(i-1)/d}\): Rotary base frequency for dimension component \(i\).
  • \(d\): Total vector dimension.
  • \(R_{\theta_i, m}\): 2D orthogonal rotation matrix applied to components of query and key vectors.
usage: Formula: \[R_{\Theta, m}^d = \text{diag}\left( R_{\theta_1, m}, R_{\theta_2, m}, \dots, R_{\theta_{d/2}, m} \right) \quad \text{where} \quad R_{\theta_i, m} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos m\theta_i & -\sin m\theta_i \\ \sin m\theta_i & \cos m\theta_i \end{pmatrix}\]

text: Ecuación para {{c1::Root Mean Square Normalization (RMSNorm)}}: \[\text{RMSNorm}(x) = \frac{x}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{d} \sum_{i=1}^d x_i^2 + \epsilon}} \odot \gamma\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(x\): Input activation vector.
  • \(d\): Dimension of the activation vector.
  • \(\epsilon\): Numerical stability offset.
  • \(\gamma\): Learnable scale parameter vector (initialized to 1).
  • \(\odot\): Hadamard (element-wise) product.
spanish: Fórmula de Root Mean Square Normalization (RMSNorm)

text:
Pregunta: Why does Pre-Layer Normalization (Pre-LN) stabilize training of extremely deep Transformers compared to Post-Layer Normalization (Post-LN)?
Opciones:
  • A) Pre-LN normalizes inputs to residual branches, preventing gradient bounds from exploding as depth increases, allowing training without warm-up
  • B) Pre-LN scales down learning rates automatically using Adam optimizer momentum updates
  • C) Post-LN forces activations to zero, causing vanishing gradients in initial layers
  • D) Pre-LN dynamically shifts weights from float32 to float16 during backward passes

Respuesta correcta: {{c1::A) Pre-LN normalizes inputs to residual branches, preventing gradient bounds from exploding as depth increases, allowing training without warm-up}}
explanation: Justificación: In Post-LN, layer normalization is placed after the residual addition ($x_{l+1} = \text{LN}(x_l + F(x_l))$), causing gradient magnitudes to grow exponentially with depth. In Pre-LN ($x_{l+1} = x_l + F(\text{LN}(x_l))$), gradients flow directly through identity residual connections, stabilizing training for deep models.
usage: Quiz assertion: A) Pre-LN normalizes inputs to residual branches, preventing gradient bounds from exploding as depth increases, allowing training without warm-up
spanish: Quiz: Why does Pre-Layer Normalization (Pre-LN) stabilize training of extremely deep Transformers compared to Post-Layer Normalization (Post-LN)?

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (DeepSeek-V3 MLA Low-Rank Key-Value Compression)
text: Ecuación para {{c1::DeepSeek-V3 MLA Low-Rank Key-Value Compression}}: \[d_c \ll d \quad \text{and} \quad c_t = W^{DKV} h_t\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(h_t\): Hidden state vector at step \(t\).
  • \(c_t\): Low-rank compressed latent key-value representation vector of size \(d_c\).
  • \(W^{DKV}\): Down-projection matrix mapping \(d\)-dim hidden state to \(d_c\)-dim.
  • \(d_c\): Latent compression dimension (MLA uses \(d_c = 512\), significantly compressing key-value cache size).
spanish: Fórmula de DeepSeek-V3 MLA Low-Rank Key-Value Compression

text: ¿Cómo funciona la estructura de {{c1::Ring Attention Context Distribution}} en el sistema?
explanation:
Ring Attention divides sequence lengths into blocks across GPU workers. While query blocks are held locally, key/value blocks are shifted in a circular ring path across GPUs to compute multi-head attention without memory aggregation bottlenecks.

graph LR GPU0["GPU 0
(Blocks Q0, K0, V0)"] -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU1["GPU 1
(Blocks Q1, K1, V1)"] GPU1 -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU2["GPU 2
(Blocks Q2, K2, V2)"] GPU2 -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU0
usage:
Visual model for Ring Attention Context Distribution.
Graph:
graph LR GPU0["GPU 0
(Blocks Q0, K0, V0)"] -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU1["GPU 1
(Blocks Q1, K1, V1)"] GPU1 -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU2["GPU 2
(Blocks Q2, K2, V2)"] GPU2 -- "Send Keys/Values" --> GPU0
spanish: Ring Attention divide la longitud de secuencia en bloques entre GPUs. Las consultas se guardan localmente, mientras que las claves/valores rotan en anillo circular entre GPUs para calcular atención sin cuellos de botella de memoria.
